channel.title: Quantum Crier
channel.description: Daily curated quantum computing news and insights by Mark Eatherly

item.title: EPB Joins Southeastern Quantum Collaborative to Support Regional Infrastructure Integration
item.description:
Curator's Take: This development signals a significant shift toward regional quantum ecosystems in the United States, with telecommunications infrastructure providers like EPB recognizing their critical role in quantum network deployment. The Southeastern Quantum Collaborative's mix of major quantum companies (IBM, D-Wave, IonQ) alongside academic institutions creates a compelling model for coordinated quantum development that could accelerate both research and practical implementation across the region. EPB's participation is particularly noteworthy given their advanced fiber infrastructure, which will be essential for future quantum communication networks and distributed quantum computing architectures. This regional approach to quantum development could serve as a blueprint for other areas looking to build competitive quantum technology clusters rather than leaving innovation concentrated in traditional tech hubs.

EPB has joined the Southeastern Quantum Collaborative (SQC) as an inaugural member. Led by the University of Alabama in Huntsville (UAH), the SQC is a regional association of academic, industrial, and government organizations—including IBM, D-Wave, and IonQ—tasked with coordinating quantum information science research and workforce development across the Southeast. EPB’s role in the collaborative involves [...] The post EPB Joins Southeastern Quantum Collaborative to Support Regional Infrastructure Integration appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Q-Day Just Got Closer: Three Papers in Three Months Are Rewriting the Quantum Threat Timeline
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights a concerning acceleration in the quantum threat to current encryption methods, with recent research dramatically reducing the estimated quantum resources needed to break widely-used cryptographic systems. The convergence of three significant papers in such a short timeframe suggests that the cryptographic community's assumptions about how long we have to transition to post-quantum cryptography may have been overly optimistic. Organizations that have been taking a wait-and-see approach to implementing quantum-resistant encryption should seriously reconsider their timelines, as the gap between theoretical vulnerability and practical quantum capability appears to be narrowing faster than previously anticipated. While we're still likely years away from cryptographically relevant quantum computers, these developments underscore the urgency of beginning post-quantum cryptography migrations now rather than later.

The quantum resources needed to break modern encryption have dropped by an order of magnitude since May 2025. Here is what happened, what it means, and what organizations should do about it. In fewer than twelve months, three research papers have sharply reduced the estimated quantum resources required to break the cryptographic systems that protect […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: STV Group and Post-Quantum Test Quantum-Secure Drone Communications
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough marks a significant milestone in the practical deployment of post-quantum cryptography, moving beyond laboratory demonstrations to real-world military applications where security is paramount. With drones increasingly becoming prime targets for electronic warfare and interception, implementing quantum-resistant encryption represents a crucial evolution in battlefield communications security. The timing is particularly relevant as military strategists worldwide grapple with protecting autonomous systems against both current cyber threats and future quantum computer attacks that could potentially break today's encryption standards. This successful field testing demonstrates that post-quantum cryptographic solutions are mature enough for deployment in the most demanding environments, likely accelerating adoption across both defense and civilian sectors.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — STV Group, an European defence technology integrator with platforms deployed in Ukraine and allied theatres, and Post-Quantum, the UK cybersecurity company today announced successful testing of the world’s first quantum-safe drones for active deployment across allied theatres. As allied nations adapt to war-fighting defined by autonomous systems and drones, the […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: UK Launches SPOQC Satellite for Quantum Communications Research
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article marks a significant milestone in the global race to establish quantum-secure communication networks, as the UK becomes one of the few nations to deploy dedicated quantum communication satellites. SPOQC represents a crucial step toward building the quantum internet infrastructure of the future, where satellite-based quantum key distribution could enable unhackable communications across continents. The mission builds on years of theoretical groundwork and laboratory experiments, now transitioning quantum communications from terrestrial fiber networks to the more challenging but potentially transformative realm of space-based systems. This development positions the UK as a serious competitor to China's quantum satellite programs and could accelerate the timeline for practical global quantum communication networks.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — A landmark space mission built on UK research excellence is set to accelerate progress in quantum communications, as the Heriot-Watt University led Integrated Quantum Networks (IQN) Hub launches the pioneering “Satellite Platform for Optical Quantum Communications” (SPOQC). Emerging from research and development efforts led by the preceding Quantum Communications Hub […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: QuSecure Joins NIST NCCoE Consortium for Post-Quantum Cryptography Migration
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights a crucial milestone in the practical deployment of post-quantum cryptography as the cybersecurity industry moves from theoretical preparation to real-world implementation. QuSecure's participation in NIST's consortium represents the kind of public-private collaboration essential for addressing the massive logistical challenge of upgrading cryptographic infrastructure before quantum computers can break current encryption methods. The focus on migration challenges rather than just algorithm development signals that the quantum cryptography transition has entered a new phase where organizations must grapple with the practical realities of replacing decades-old security systems. This consortium approach could accelerate adoption by providing standardized migration frameworks that organizations desperately need as the quantum threat timeline continues to compress.

QuSecure has announced its participation in the Migration to Post-Quantum Cryptography Project consortium, organized by the National Cybersecurity Center of Excellence (NCCoE). This collaboration is designed to assist organizations in transitioning from current public-key algorithms to NIST-standardized quantum-resistant alternatives. The project addresses the technical and operational challenges associated with replacing legacy cryptography that is vulnerable [...] The post QuSecure Joins NIST NCCoE Consortium for Post-Quantum Cryptography Migration appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: High-fidelity entangled photon pairs from a quantum-dot-based single-photon source
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research represents a significant breakthrough in addressing one of quantum networking's most fundamental challenges: creating high-quality entangled photon pairs that can actually work in real-world quantum networks. While traditional sources based on spontaneous parametric down-conversion suffer from an unavoidable trade-off between brightness and unwanted multi-photon noise, these quantum dot sources achieve an impressive 96% fidelity while suppressing the multi-photon events that plague existing systems. The key innovation lies in generating "swappable" photons that are indistinguishable in all the right ways, enabling efficient entanglement swapping between network nodes - a critical requirement for scaling quantum networks beyond simple point-to-point connections. This work positions semiconductor quantum dots as serious contenders to replace current photon sources in future quantum internet infrastructure, potentially offering both higher fidelity and better scalability for practical quantum communication systems.

Entangled photon pairs are a ubiquitous resource in quantum technologies, used in quantum key distribution and quantum networking as well as fundamental tests of non-locality. For scalable quantum networks, pairs that are indistinguishable in all unentangled degrees of freedom are essential, as they enable high-fidelity entanglement swapping across network nodes. To date the most-studied sources of "swappable" entangled photon pairs have been based on spontaneous parametric down-conversion (SPDC) in non-linear crystals. However, the probabilistic nature and unavoidable trade-off between brightness and unwanted multi-photon emission limits their performance in lossy channels. Here, we demonstrate a high-fidelity source of "swappable" entangled photon pairs using a semiconductor quantum dot (QD) coupled to a tunable microcavity. By actively modulating the QD emission between orthogonal polarisation states, delaying one path in a low-loss Herriott cell, and recombining the two on a balanced beam splitter, we generate entangled photon pairs with a fidelity of $96.1\pm0.5$ %. We identify and mitigate fidelity-limiting factors, achieving a maximum fidelity of $98.1\pm0.5$ % through time-resolved post-selection. The scheme suppresses residual multi-photon events concentrated near the excitation pulse and has only a modest impact on the rate. Furthermore, the photons are mutually indistinguishable, enabling efficient entanglement swapping. Our results establish semiconductor QDs as a viable platform for quantum network-compatible swappable entangled photon pair generation, with feasible entanglement generation rates exceeding 0.5 Gpairs/s.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Four Generations of Quantum Biomedical Sensors
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article provides a valuable roadmap for quantum sensing in healthcare by categorizing quantum biosensors into four distinct generations based on their quantum sophistication, from basic energy-level detection to fully integrated quantum sensing-computing systems. The framework helps clarify why current quantum sensors haven't yet transformed medicine despite their theoretical advantages, highlighting practical challenges like classical noise limits and the need for sensors to work in close proximity to biological tissue. Most intriguingly, the proposed fourth generation envisions quantum sensors that don't just detect signals but directly process biological information using quantum machine learning, potentially enabling real-time diagnostic capabilities that classical sensors simply cannot match. This systematic approach could accelerate the translation of quantum sensing from laboratory demonstrations to actual clinical applications where ultra-sensitive detection of biomarkers, neural activity, or cellular processes could revolutionize medical diagnostics.

Quantum sensing technologies offer transformative potential for ultra-sensitive biomedical sensing, yet their clinical translation remains constrained by classical noise limits and a reliance on macroscopic ensembles. We propose a unifying generational framework to organize the evolving landscape of quantum biosensors based on their utilization of quantum resources. First-generation devices utilize discrete energy levels for signal transduction but follow classical scaling laws. Second-generation sensors exploit quantum coherence to reach the standard quantum limit, while third-generation architectures leverage entanglement and spin squeezing to approach Heisenberg-limited precision. We further define an emerging fourth generation characterized by the end-to-end integration of quantum sensing with quantum learning and variational circuits, enabling adaptive inference directly within the quantum domain. By analyzing critical parameters such as bandwidth matching and sensor-tissue proximity, we identify key technological bottlenecks and propose a roadmap for transitioning from measuring physical observables to extracting structured biological information with quantum-enhanced intelligence.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Research Team Finds Useful Quantum Computers Could Be Built with as Few as 10,000 Qubits
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research represents a potentially game-changing shift in quantum computing's timeline, as most experts have estimated that fault-tolerant quantum computers would need hundreds of thousands or even millions of qubits to be practically useful. The collaboration between Caltech theorists and experimentalists at Oratomic suggests they've found novel error correction approaches that could dramatically reduce these requirements, bringing useful quantum advantage within reach of current technological capabilities. If validated, this 10,000-qubit threshold could mean we're looking at commercially viable quantum computers within the next decade rather than waiting 20-30 years. The fact that this comes from both academic research and a startup with skin in the game adds credibility to what could be the most optimistic realistic timeline we've seen for practical quantum computing.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Quantum computers of the future may be closer to reality thanks to new research from Caltech and Oratomic, a Caltech-linked start-up company. Theorists and experimentalists teamed up to develop a new approach for reducing the errors that riddle today’s rudimentary quantum computers. Whereas these machines were previously thought to require […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Dynamics of entanglement entropy for a locally monitored lattice gauge theory
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles a fundamental challenge in quantum simulation of gauge theories - understanding how continuous measurements affect quantum entanglement in these complex systems. The finding that local monitoring doesn't trigger measurement-induced phase transitions is particularly significant because it suggests that certain quantum simulations of particle physics models can maintain their coherent properties even under realistic experimental conditions where measurements are unavoidable. This work provides crucial benchmarking insights for quantum computers attempting to simulate lattice gauge theories, which represent some of the most promising applications for demonstrating quantum advantage in fundamental physics research. The results help establish the robustness of these quantum simulations against measurement-induced decoherence, bringing us closer to reliable quantum exploration of high-energy physics phenomena.

The $1+1$ dimensional $Z_2$ gauge theory is the simplest model that allows for quantum computation or quantum simulation to probe the fundamental aspects of a gauge theory coupled with dynamical fermions. To reliably benchmark such a system, it is crucial to understand the non-unitary quantum dynamics arising from the underlying non-Hermitian evolution and to model the effects of quantum measurements. This work focuses on monitoring ultra-local physical observables for a $\mathbb Z_2$ gauge theory. Tensor network calculations are performed to dynamically probe entanglement entropy at larger lattice sizes. In this work, we report that continuously monitoring local and diagonal observables (electric and mass energy densities) in the computational basis demonstrates the absence of any measurement-induced phase transition, as indicated by the system-size independence of the late-time saturation value of the bipartite entanglement entropy.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Topological sum rule for geometric phases of quantum gates
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article establishes a fascinating mathematical bridge between abstract topology and practical quantum gate operations, showing that the geometric phases accumulated during two-qubit gates are fundamentally linked to the topological properties of their underlying Hamiltonians. The key insight is that quantum gates from different topological classes must distribute these phases differently in measurable ways, providing a new theoretical framework for understanding and potentially optimizing quantum operations. Most intriguingly, the work proves that nontrivial topology is actually a necessary condition for generating entanglement, meaning only Hamiltonians with specific topological characteristics can create the quantum correlations essential for quantum computing. This connection between topology and entanglement could inform the design of more robust quantum gates and help explain why certain physical implementations are naturally better at maintaining quantum coherence.

We establish a topological sum rule, $ν_U = \frac{1}{2π}\sum_nγ_n = 2mν_H$, connecting the geometric phases accumulated by a two-qubit system over a complete basis of initial states to the winding number $ν_H$ classifying its Hamiltonian. Implementations of the same gate from different topological classes must distribute these phases differently, making their distinction measurable through the Wootters concurrence. As a corollary, nontrivial topology is a necessary condition for entanglement: only Hamiltonians with access to $ν_H \neq 0$ can generate it.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: QuSecure Collaborating with NIST’s National Cybersecurity Center of Excellence to Address Post-Quantum Algorithm Migration
item.description:
Curator's Take: This collaboration between QuSecure and NIST's National Cybersecurity Center of Excellence represents a crucial step toward preparing the world's digital infrastructure for the quantum computing threat to current encryption methods. As quantum computers advance toward breaking widely-used RSA and elliptic curve cryptography, organizations need practical guidance on migrating to quantum-resistant algorithms that NIST has already standardized. The partnership highlights how post-quantum cryptography is moving from theoretical concern to urgent implementation challenge, requiring specialized tools and methodologies to ensure smooth transitions without compromising security. This work will likely produce valuable best practices that could accelerate enterprise adoption of quantum-safe encryption before large-scale quantum computers make current cryptographic systems vulnerable.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — QuSecure™, Inc., the market leader in post-quantum cybersecurity and cryptographic agility, today announced it is collaborating with the National Cybersecurity Center of Excellence (NCCoE) in the Migration to Post-Quantum Cryptography Project Consortium to bring awareness to the issues involved in migrating to post-quantum algorithms and to develop practices to ease migration from current public-key […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: London Tech Week 2026 to Showcase Global Deep Tech Innovators in Space, Robotics, Sciences, Quantum and AI
item.description:
Curator's Take: This announcement signals London's growing ambition to position itself as a major quantum computing hub alongside established players like Silicon Valley and Boston. London Tech Week 2026's dedicated Deep Tech Stage reflects how quantum computing has evolved from a purely academic pursuit to a technology with clear commercial and strategic implications that governments and industry leaders are actively pursuing. The timing is particularly significant as the UK continues to invest heavily in quantum research through initiatives like the National Quantum Computing Centre, making this event a potential showcase for British quantum achievements on the global stage. For the quantum community, such high-profile platforms help bridge the gap between cutting-edge research and practical applications by bringing together researchers, investors, and industry leaders in one venue.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — London Tech Week 2026 (8-12 June 2026) has announced its dedicated Deep Tech Stage, exploring the most exciting breakthroughs in space, robotics, materials, life sciences, AI and quantum computing. At a time when governments and industry are racing to turn scientific breakthroughs into economic and strategic advantage, the programme is […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Optimal Control of Spin Squeezing in 2D Finite-Range Interacting Systems
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of the key bottlenecks in quantum sensing: generating strong spin squeezing in realistic systems with finite-range interactions, which has proven much more challenging than in idealized all-to-all connected models. The breakthrough here is demonstrating that a simple, experimentally feasible control strategy using just a single collective magnetic field can dramatically enhance squeezing performance, even surpassing theoretical benchmarks like two-axis twisting. What makes this particularly exciting is the scalability of their approach - they've developed computational methods that can handle much larger 2D systems than previously possible, while also proving the protocol works under realistic conditions including noise and open boundaries. This could significantly advance quantum-enhanced sensing applications in atomic systems and trapped ion platforms, where achieving strong entanglement for precision measurements remains a major experimental challenge.

Spin squeezing serves as both a fundamental witness of quantum entanglement and a critical resource for quantum-enhanced metrology. While generating substantial spin squeezing in finite-range interacting systems remains challenging, such capability is important for advancing quantum technologies. In this work, we develop an optimal control strategy for achieving enhanced spin squeezing in a two-dimensional XX model with dipolar interactions. Leveraging rotor-spin-wave theory for periodic boundary conditions, we circumvent computational bottlenecks to explore control strategies at unprecedented scales. Remarkably, optimizing a single collective transverse field is sufficient to achieve substantial squeezing enhancement, exceeding the two-axis-twisting benchmark. The optimized control field achieves this breakthrough by dynamically suppressing inter-subspace mixing induced by the finite-range interactions, thereby confining the system evolution predominantly within the maximal spin subspace. We further extend rotor-spin-wave theory to open boundary conditions and incorporate dephasing noise, providing a scalable framework for realistic systems. Under these conditions, the optimized protocol remains effective, highlighting its robustness and suitability for experimental implementation.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Monarch Quantum Surpasses $100 Million in Capital and Contracts with $55 Million Growth Round to Scale Quantum Photonics Infrastructure
item.description:
Curator's Take: This significant funding milestone demonstrates the growing investor confidence in photonic quantum computing approaches, with Monarch Quantum's rapid ascent from founding in 2025 to surpassing $100 million in combined capital and contracts highlighting the accelerating commercialization timeline in quantum tech. The involvement of 55 North, the world's largest dedicated quantum fund, alongside other deep tech investors signals strong institutional backing for photonics-based quantum infrastructure, which offers potential advantages in terms of room-temperature operation and networking capabilities compared to superconducting approaches. The "oversubscribed" nature of this growth round reflects the intense competition among investors to back promising quantum hardware companies as the field moves toward practical applications. This funding will likely accelerate Monarch's development of integrated photonics platforms that could play a crucial role in scaling quantum computing and quantum communication networks.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Monarch Quantum, a developer of integrated photonics hardware for quantum technologies, today announced a $55 million oversubscribed growth round, led by Serendipity Capital, a leading deep tech investor, with participation from 55 North, the world’s largest dedicated pure play quantum fund, and Global Innovation Labs. Founded in 2025, Monarch entered […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: The Manipulate-and-Observe Attack on Quantum Key Distribution
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research exposes a critical vulnerability in quantum key distribution systems that challenges the widely-held belief that QKD provides unconditional security. The newly identified "Manipulate-and-Observe" attack cleverly exploits the classical post-processing phase of QKD, showing how an adversary can inject errors just below detection thresholds during quantum transmission, then use information leaked during the reconciliation process to dramatically narrow down possible keys. What makes this particularly concerning is that the attack targets BB84 and Cascade protocols that are fundamental to current QKD implementations, potentially reducing security from theoretically unbreakable to practically vulnerable. This work highlights that quantum cryptography's security depends not just on quantum mechanics but on the entire protocol stack, emphasizing the need for more robust reconciliation methods that minimize information leakage.

Quantum key distribution is often regarded as an unconditionally secure method to exchange a secret key by harnessing fundamental aspects of quantum mechanics. Despite the robustness of key exchange, classical post-processing reveals vulnerabilities that an eavesdropper could target. In particular, many reconciliation protocols correct errors by comparing the parities of subsets between both parties. These communications occur over insecure channels, leaking information that an eavesdropper could exploit. Currently there is no holistic threat model that addresses how parity-leakage during reconciliation might be actively manipulated. In this paper we introduce a new form of attack, namely the Manipulate-and-Observe attack in which the adversary (1) partially intercepts a fraction $ρ$ of the qubits during key exchange, injecting the maximally tolerated amount of errors up to the 11 percent error threshold whilst remaining undetected and (2) probes the maximum amount of parity-leakage during reconciliation, and exploits it using a vectorised, parallel brute force filter to shrink the search space from 2n down to as few as a single candidate, for an n-bit reconciled key. We perform simulations of the attack, deploying it on the most widely used protocol, BB84, andthe benchmark reconciliation protocol, Cascade. Our simulation results demonstrate that the attack can significantly reduce the security below the theoretical bound and, in the worst case, fully recover the reconciled key material. The principles of the attack could threaten other parity-based reconciliation schemes, like Low Density Parity Check, which underscores the need for urgent consideration of the combined security of key exchange and post-processing.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: memQ Raises $10 Million in Series A
item.description:
Curator's Take: This funding round highlights the growing recognition that quantum networking infrastructure is just as crucial as quantum processors themselves for realizing distributed quantum computing's full potential. memQ's focus on quantum networking solutions addresses a critical bottleneck in scaling quantum systems beyond single machines, enabling quantum computers to work together across distances and potentially unlocking applications that require more qubits than any single device can provide. The strong investor interest from quantum-focused funds like Quantonation signals that the industry is maturing beyond just hardware development to encompass the entire quantum computing stack. This investment positions memQ to help build the "quantum internet" backbone that will be essential for connecting quantum devices and creating truly distributed quantum computing networks.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — memQ™, the industry leader in quantum networking solutions for distributed quantum computing, announced today the closing of its $10M Series A round of financing. The round was co-led by Quantonation and Ocean Azul Partners, with syndicate participation from both existing and new investors. Quantum computing is the next major shift in computing, forecasted […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Entanglement in prepare-and-measure scenarios without receiver inputs
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles a fundamental puzzle in quantum communication: how can you achieve quantum advantages in the simplest possible scenarios where the receiver has no control over what measurements to perform? The work reveals that even in these stripped-down setups, entanglement combined with adaptive measurements can still provide genuine quantum benefits, with the most dramatic advantages emerging when high-dimensional entangled states are used alongside non-projective measurements. Particularly intriguing is the finding that non-projective measurements—often considered secondary in quantum experiments—actually play an indispensable role in unlocking quantum advantages when quantum messages are involved. This research not only advances our theoretical understanding of quantum communication protocols but also provides practical blueprints for certifying quantum entanglement in black-box scenarios where you can't fully trust the measurement devices.

The most elementary prepare-and-measure scenarios have no independent measurement inputs. No inputs mean that quantum advantages require two indispensable ingredients: shared entanglement and measurements that can be adapted to the communicated messages. Understanding these scenarios is therefore conceptually natural, but also practically relevant, since they act as testbeds for black-box certification of adaptive one-way LOCC. Here, we study them systematically and reveal several of their basic features. For classical messages, we first identify the minimal scenario with a quantum advantage and show that it is maximised by high-dimensional entanglement. Then, we identify the next-to-minimal scenario, and show that quantum advantages can be propelled by nonlocality of the Clauser-Horne-Shimony-Holt type, which makes this an appropriate setting for certification experiments. Proceeding further, we replace classical messages with quantum messages, but require the receiver to read the message before measuring the entangled particle. We show that this leads to amplified quantum advantages, that are made possible only thanks to non-projective message read-out. This in dispensable role of non-projective measurements challenges the common wisdom that they play a secondary role in revealing the power of quantum correlations in black-box experiments.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Logical-to-Physical Compilation for Reducing Depth in Distributed Quantum Systems
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article tackles one of the most pressing challenges in scaling up quantum computing: how to efficiently connect multiple quantum processors into larger, more powerful systems without drowning in communication overhead. The researchers developed a clever compiler that identifies opportunities to execute multiple quantum gates in parallel across distributed systems, rather than forcing them to run sequentially as previous approaches required. This work is particularly significant because it addresses the depth problem that has plagued distributed quantum computing - where the time needed for quantum processors to communicate and share entanglement often destroys the fragile quantum states before useful computation can complete. By keeping circuits shallow while maintaining logical correctness, this compiler brings us closer to practical large-scale quantum systems that could tackle problems beyond what today's isolated quantum processors can handle.

Quantum computing is expected to become a foundational technology for solving problems that exceed the capabilities of classical systems. As quantum algorithms and hardware technologies continue to advance, the need for scalable architectures becomes increasingly clear. Distributed quantum computing offers a promising path forward by interconnecting multiple smaller processors into a larger, more powerful system. However, distributed quantum computing introduces significant circuit depth overhead, as logical operations are typically decomposed into sequential physical procedures that require entanglement generation. These sequential operations limit the reliability of quantum algorithms in the NISQ era due to noise. In this work, we present a compiler that integrates logical-to-physical decomposition with depth-aware rescheduling to reduce the execution cost of distributed quantum circuits. The compiler identifies sequences of logical CNOT gates that share a control or target qubit, reschedules them into parallel instruction groups, and applies decompositions that allow multiple gates to be executed simultaneously using distributed shared entanglement resources. An algorithm is proposed that ensures parallelism is created when possible while keeping logical equivalence and that circuit depth is never increased. Benchmark results demonstrate that the compiler consistently reduces circuit depth for circuits containing inherently sequential CNOT structures, while leaving already-parallel circuits unchanged. These results highlight the value of combining scheduling and hardware-aware decomposition, and establish the compiler as a practical tool for improving the fidelity of distributed quantum computations.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Non-perturbative CPMG scaling and qutrit-driven breakdown under compiled superconducting-qubit control: a single-qubit study
item.description:
Curator's Take: This study reveals a fascinating breakdown in our understanding of how quantum error correction sequences behave in real superconducting qubits when pushed beyond the simple two-level approximation. The researchers discovered that CPMG pulse sequences - a workhorse technique for extending qubit coherence - can actually exhibit chaotic, non-monotonic behavior when the qubit's third energy level becomes active, leading to unexpected population asymmetries and partial coherence revival that existing theoretical models completely miss. What makes this particularly valuable is that the team developed a comprehensive simulation framework that bridges the gap between actual quantum computer instruction sets and the complex physics of multi-level qubits interacting with realistic noise environments. The findings provide experimentally testable predictions that could fundamentally reshape how we design and optimize quantum error correction protocols for near-term quantum processors.

Decoherence in superconducting qubits emerges from the interplay of multilevel dynamics and structured environmental noise, yet perturbative models cannot capture all resulting signatures. Here, EmuPlat couples instruction-set-architecture-level waveform generation to the hierarchical equations of motion (HEOM) under $1/f$ non-Markovian pure dephasing. In the resulting non-perturbative regime -- where filter-function predictions become quantitatively uninformative -- CPMG scaling of a three-level superconducting transmon yields one calibration result, two physical findings, and one structural null. Y-CPMG exhibits axis-dependent scaling-law breakdown -- non-monotonic decoherence, partial coherence revival, and pronounced X--Y population asymmetry ($0.204$ vs ${<}\,0.01$) -- driven by third-level anharmonicity amplified by bath memory; X-CPMG maintains well-behaved power-law scaling with a finite-$n$ transient excess consistent with non-Markovian bath-memory effects. The structural null is equally informative: waveform-level differences -- Standard versus VPPU realizations -- remain undetectable across all coupling strengths, establishing that rotating-frame pure-dephasing coupling renders control-layer detail invisible to scaling observables. These findings define testable predictions, the most experimentally accessible requiring only qualitative verification.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum Sensing with Triplet Pair States: A Theoretical Study
item.description:
Curator's Take: This theoretical study explores an intriguing alternative to conventional quantum sensors by harnessing the unique properties of pentacene dimers, where two molecules work together to create entangled triplet pair states through singlet fission. While individual pentacene molecules have already shown promise as quantum sensors, the researchers demonstrate that these coupled systems could offer superior performance when detecting small groups of nuclear spins, potentially opening new avenues for nanoscale magnetic field sensing. The work is particularly significant because it provides the theoretical foundation for using multi-excitonic molecular states as tunable quantum probes, suggesting that carefully engineered organic molecules could compete with or complement existing quantum sensing platforms like nitrogen-vacancy centers in diamond. The finding that sensitivity scales with the number of control pulses and performs best at low magnetic fields gives experimentalists clear guidance for optimizing these molecular quantum sensors in practical applications.

Molecular quantum sensors represent a promising frontier for the detection of nuclear magnetic resonance signals and alternating current magnetic fields at the nanoscale, potentially reaching single-proton sensitivity. Although the triplet states of molecular pentacene provide a viable sensing architecture, the triplet pair states produced by singlet fission of pentacene dimers could enable more flexible quantum manipulations through entanglement. In this work, we model the quantum sensing efficacy of a spin-polarized quintet manifold in a photoexcited pentacene dimer generated via intramolecular singlet fission. Using a Lindblad master equation approach, we simulate the evolution of the triplet pair state under standard dynamical decoupling sequences, including spin echo, XY4, and XY8 and provide a direct performance comparison to the traditional pentacene monomer benchmark. While both architectures exhibit comparable sensitivity for isolated single-spin detection, our findings indicate that the dimer architecture provides a superior interaction cross-section for detecting small ensembles of nuclear spins. Analytical expressions derived for fluorescence modulation demonstrate that sensitivity is optimized in the low-magnetic field regime and scales with the number of pulses in the sensing protocol. This study establishes a theoretical baseline for utilizing high-spin multi-excitonic states as chemically tunable, high-sensitivity quantum probes.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: PAEMS: Precise and Adaptive Error Model for Superconducting Quantum Processors
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles a fundamental bottleneck in quantum error correction: the chicken-and-egg problem of needing massive error data to build QEC systems, but being unable to generate that data due to current hardware limitations. PAEMS represents a significant advance by creating more accurate synthetic error models that can capture the complex, real-world behavior of superconducting qubits across both space and time, moving beyond oversimplified depolarizing models that miss crucial error correlations. The dramatic improvements demonstrated across multiple major quantum platforms - including substantial accuracy gains over Google's established SI1000 model - suggest this could accelerate the development of practical quantum error correction by providing researchers with much better training data for their decoders. This kind of precise error modeling is essential groundwork for the fault-tolerant quantum computers needed to solve real-world problems.

Superconducting quantum processor units (QPUs) are incapable of producing massive datasets for quantum error correction (QEC) because of hardware limitations. Thus, QEC decoders heavily depend on synthetic data from qubit error models. Classic depolarizing error models with polynomial complexity present limited accuracy. Coherent density matrix methods suffer from exponential complexity $\propto O(4^n)$ where $n$ represents the number of qubits. This paper introduces PAEMS: a precise and adaptive qubit error model. Its qubit-wise separation framework, incorporating leakage propagation, captures error evolvements crossing spatial and temporal domains. Utilizing repetition-code experiment datasets, PAEMS effectively identifies the intrinsic qubit errors through an end-to-end optimization pipeline. Experiments on IBM's QPUs have demonstrated a 19.5$\times$, 9.3$\times$, and 5.2$\times$ reduction in timelike, spacelike, and spacetime error correlation, respectively, surpassing all of the previous works. It also outperforms the accuracy of Google's SI1000 error model by 58$\sim$73\% on multiple quantum platforms, including IBM's Brisbane, Sherbrooke, and Torino, as well as China Mobile's Wuyue and QuantumCTek's Tianyan.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Multipartite controlled-NOT gates using molecules and Rydberg atoms
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research demonstrates a clever hybrid approach that combines the complementary strengths of two promising quantum platforms: polar molecules provide stable, long-lived qubits while Rydberg atoms contribute their exceptionally strong interactions for rapid gate operations. The ability to implement multipartite CNOT gates, where multiple qubits can simultaneously control or be controlled by others, represents a significant step toward more efficient quantum circuits that could reduce the overhead typically required for complex quantum algorithms. Achieving over 99% fidelity for four-qubit operations while maintaining robustness against decoherence suggests this hybrid architecture could offer practical advantages for near-term quantum computing applications. The scalable nature of this approach particularly stands out, as most quantum platforms struggle to maintain high performance as system size increases.

We propose high-fidelity controlled-NOT (CNOT) gates in a hybrid system of polar molecules and Rydberg atoms based on the unconventional Rydberg pumping mechanism. By combining the rich internal structure of polar molecules with the strong dipole-dipole interactions of Rydberg atoms, we realize both two-to-one and one-to-two gate configurations. Numerical simulations show that the gate performance is robust against spontaneous emission from Rydberg states. The approach naturally extends to larger systems, as demonstrated by four-qubit implementations achieving three-to-one and one-to-three CNOT gates with fidelities exceeding 99\%. These results highlight hybrid molecule-Rydberg atom architectures as a promising platform for scalable quantum information processing.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Entanglement between an NV Center and Chiral Photons in a Topological SWCNT Plasmonic Microtoroid
item.description:
Curator's Take: This theoretical proposal presents an ingenious approach to one of quantum networking's biggest challenges: creating high-fidelity, controllable connections between stationary quantum memories and flying photonic qubits. By leveraging the exotic physics of carbon nanotube plasmonics and topological effects, the researchers show how an NV center's spin could be deterministically mapped onto chiral photons with tunable frequencies controlled by magnetic flux. The work is particularly exciting because it addresses a key bottleneck in quantum networks - the typically random nature of photon emission from solid-state qubits - while introducing topological protection that could enhance system robustness. Although still theoretical, this hybrid platform could represent a significant step toward practical quantum repeaters and distributed quantum computing architectures if the proposed cryogenic implementation proves experimentally viable.

We present a theoretical proposal for a hybrid solid-state quantum node based on a single nitrogen-vacancy (NV) center coupled to a topological single-walled carbon nanotube (SWCNT) plasmonic microtoroid. The SWCNT ring supports deeply sub-wavelength whispering-gallery-like plasmonic modes that are naturally described within a Tomonaga-Luttinger liquid framework. Owing to the closed-ring topology, the cavity spectrum contains a zero-mode sector that is tunable by an external magnetic flux through an Aharonov-Bohm shift. We show that the strongly confined CNT near field can exhibit chiral spin-momentum locking, enabling the two circularly polarized NV transitions to couple selectively to clockwise and counter-clockwise cavity modes, while the parasitic linearly polarized $π$-transition is strongly suppressed by the pronounced anisotropy of the local Purcell enhancement. Based on a tripod stimulated Raman adiabatic passage (STIRAP) scheme, the system can in principle map the NV spin onto a spin-photon entangled state in a deterministic manner, which is then emitted into a side-coupled tapered optical fiber as a tunable flying qubit. We derive the cavity spectrum, the chiral selection rules, the effective tripod Hamiltonian, and the open-system master equation. Quantitative estimates indicate that, under cryogenic conditions and in the overcoupled regime, high-fidelity spin-photon entanglement and in situ magnetic tuning of the emitted photon frequency are in principle achievable. We also discuss a realistic fabrication route for the CNT resonator and deterministic positioning strategies for a single NV center in the CNT near field.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Direct measurement of the energy spectrum of a quantum dot qubit
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough represents a major advance in characterizing quantum dot qubits, providing the first comprehensive method to map out the complete energy landscape of these crucial quantum computing building blocks. While previous techniques like DAPS could only peek at small portions of the quantum dot's behavior, this new delta-axis spectroscopy (DAXS) method reveals the full energy spectrum across all operating conditions, giving researchers an unprecedented "quantum MRI" of their qubits. The ability to extract all 15 energy levels of the Hubbard model means scientists can now predict and optimize qubit performance with far greater precision, potentially accelerating the development of more reliable silicon-based quantum processors that could scale to practical applications.

The mapping between gate voltages applied to a double quantum dot, and the parameters of a Hubbard-like Hamiltonian, is of utmost importance for understanding and operating spin qubits. State-of-the-art techniques for measuring Hamiltonian parameters (e.g., detuning axis pulsed spectroscopy, DAPS) provide details about energy levels; however, tunnel coupling estimates typically reveal only a small portion of the full Hamiltonian. Here, we demonstrate a Hamiltonian-agnostic technique for measuring the double dot energy spectrum over a wide energy range, at every value of the detuning, called delta-axis spectroscopy (DAXS). We apply the DAXS method to obtain the energy spectrum of a Si/SiGe double quantum dot and use this data to extract the diagonal and off-diagonal couplings of a 15-level Hubbard-like Hamiltonian, demonstrating very good agreement with the experimental measurements.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Podcast with Tom Darras, CEO and Co-founder, Welinq
item.description:
Curator's Take: This interview offers valuable insights into quantum networking's role as a critical enabler for scaling quantum computing beyond single processors. Welinq's focus on quantum memories and qubit-photon interfaces addresses one of the field's most challenging technical hurdles: how to reliably connect quantum processors across distances while preserving fragile quantum states. The discussion of modular scale-out clusters is particularly timely as companies like IBM and Google push toward larger quantum systems, where networking could provide an alternative path to scaling beyond the limits of monolithic quantum processors. Tom Darras provides a clear technical breakdown of the networking stack that will be essential for the distributed quantum computing architectures many believe are necessary for practical quantum advantage.

Yuval Boger interviews Tom Darras, CEO and co-founder of Welinq. They discuss how quantum networking uses shared entanglement to interconnect quantum processors, enabling modular scale-out clusters and quantum-safe connectivity between data centers. Tom explains the technical building blocks—qubit-photon interfaces, optical networks, entangled photon sources, and especially quantum memories—as well as the performance metrics that matter [...] The post Podcast with Tom Darras, CEO and Co-founder, Welinq appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: UMass Amherst and UCSB Demonstrate Photonic Chip Technology for Miniaturized Quantum Systems
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough represents a crucial step toward making quantum computers practical by dramatically shrinking the massive optical infrastructure that typically fills entire rooms down to chip-scale components. The collaboration between UMass Amherst and UCSB demonstrates how photonic integration can miniaturize trapped-ion quantum systems, which are among the most promising platforms for scalable quantum computing due to their high-fidelity operations and long coherence times. By putting lasers and optical controls directly on chips, this approach could eventually enable quantum computers that fit in data centers rather than specialized laboratory facilities, making the technology far more accessible and commercially viable. The work addresses one of the biggest engineering challenges facing trapped-ion systems and could accelerate their path from research labs to real-world applications.

Researchers from UMass Amherst and the University of California Santa Barbara (UCSB) have demonstrated an integrated "system-on-a-chip" technology that replaces room-sized laser and optical components with miniaturized photonic chips. Led by Assistant Professor Robert Niffenegger and Professor Daniel Blumenthal, the team utilized trapped-ion technology to perform qubit and clock operations on a chip-scale device. This [...] The post UMass Amherst and UCSB Demonstrate Photonic Chip Technology for Miniaturized Quantum Systems appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Silicon quantum computer performs logical operations for the first time
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough marks a pivotal moment for quantum computing as it bridges the gap between experimental quantum physics and the massive manufacturing infrastructure already built around silicon semiconductors. The demonstration of logical operations—where multiple physical qubits work together to create more robust "logical" qubits that can correct their own errors—represents a crucial step toward fault-tolerant quantum computing that could actually be manufactured at scale. Silicon's natural advantages, including impressive coherence times and seamless integration with existing chip fabrication processes, position it as a serious contender against other quantum computing approaches like superconducting circuits and trapped ions. This achievement suggests that the same foundries producing today's smartphones and processors could potentially manufacture tomorrow's quantum computers, dramatically accelerating the path to practical quantum computing applications.

Silicon is ubiquitous in modern electronics, and now it is becoming increasingly useful in quantum computing. In particular, silicon's compatibility with existing chip technology and its long coherence times in silicon-based spin qubits make it a promising material for scalable quantum computing. A new study, published in Nature Nanotechnology, has demonstrated silicon's use in a logical quantum processor, representing the first of its kind.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum computer accurately simulates real magnetic materials, reproducing national laboratory data
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights a crucial milestone where quantum computers are finally delivering on one of their most promising applications - simulating quantum materials that are too complex for classical computers to handle accurately. The fact that quantum simulations can now reproduce real experimental data from national laboratories represents a significant leap from proof-of-concept demonstrations to practical utility in materials science. This development could accelerate the discovery of new materials for everything from more efficient solar cells to revolutionary superconductors, as researchers can now explore quantum material properties computationally before investing in costly experimental synthesis. While still early-stage, this marks quantum computing's transition from theoretical promise to tangible scientific tool in one of its most natural application domains.

Studying and designing novel materials is a central application of quantum mechanics. Chemists, materials scientists, and physicists focus on subtle interactions in quantum materials and to uncover them they rely on sophisticated computational and experimental techniques. Computer simulations that connect microscopic quantum interactions to measurable material properties complement experimental data to connect structure to function—but classical computers can struggle to simulate those properties. Fortunately, scientists today have a new tool in their toolbox: quantum computers.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Unlocking scalable entanglement will enable next-generation quantum computing
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights one of quantum computing's most fundamental challenges: creating and maintaining large-scale entanglement between qubits, which is essential for quantum advantage in real-world applications. While the summary presents familiar quantum computing promises, the focus on "scalable entanglement" points to critical ongoing research into how quantum systems can maintain their delicate interconnected states as they grow beyond today's laboratory demonstrations of hundreds of qubits. The ability to reliably entangle thousands or millions of qubits will determine whether quantum computers can tackle the complex optimization and simulation problems that classical computers struggle with, making this a key battleground for achieving practical quantum supremacy. This research area is particularly timely as major quantum computing companies race to build larger, more stable quantum processors while grappling with decoherence and error rates that currently limit scalability.

Quantum computing promises to transform our world in rapid, radical and revolutionary ways: solving in seconds problems that would take classical computers years, accelerating the discovery of new medicines, creating sustainable materials, optimizing complex systems, and strengthening cybersecurity. It does so using qubits, the quantum counterparts of classical bits, which can occupy multiple states simultaneously and enable a fundamentally new kind of computation.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Dual-rail superconducting qubits generate high-fidelity logical entanglement, study finds
item.description:
Curator's Take: This study represents a significant step toward fault-tolerant quantum computing by demonstrating that dual-rail superconducting qubits can create high-quality logical entanglement between encoded quantum states. Dual-rail encoding is particularly promising because it offers built-in error protection by distributing quantum information across multiple physical components, making the logical qubits more resilient to noise and decoherence that plague current quantum systems. The achievement of high-fidelity logical entanglement is crucial because most quantum algorithms require entangled logical qubits to function properly, and maintaining this entanglement with minimal errors has been one of the major hurdles in scaling quantum computers. This work brings us closer to practical quantum computers that can run meaningful algorithms reliably, marking important progress in the race toward quantum advantage in real-world applications.

Quantum computers, systems that process information leveraging quantum mechanical effects, could outperform classical computers on some advanced tasks. These systems rely on qubits, the fundamental units of quantum information, that become linked via an effect known as quantum entanglement and share a unified quantum state.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Google Quantum AI Adopts Dual-Modality Strategy with Neutral Atom Expansion
item.description:
Curator's Take: Google's decision to pursue both superconducting and neutral atom quantum computing platforms represents a significant strategic pivot that acknowledges the fundamental uncertainties still facing the field. This dual-modality approach hedges against the possibility that superconducting qubits alone may not achieve the scale and coherence needed for practical quantum advantage, while neutral atoms offer compelling advantages in connectivity and potential for larger qubit counts. The move follows similar multi-platform strategies from other major players like Amazon and IBM, suggesting the industry is maturing beyond single-technology bets toward more diversified research portfolios. This expansion could accelerate progress by allowing Google to leverage the unique strengths of each platform while cross-pollinating insights between their hardware teams.

Google Quantum AI has announced a major strategic expansion of its research roadmap, adding neutral atom quantum computing to its established superconducting hardware program. Led by founder Hartmut Neven, the move signals a shift toward a multi-platform approach to reach commercially relevant quantum advantage. While Google remains confident in the timeline for superconducting systems by [...] The post Google Quantum AI Adopts Dual-Modality Strategy with Neutral Atom Expansion appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: New Zealand Researchers Report Advance in Optical Ising Machine for Hybrid Quantum Optimization
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research highlights an important trend in quantum computing where hybrid classical-quantum systems are emerging as practical stepping stones toward full-scale quantum advantage. Optical Ising machines represent a particularly promising approach because they can leverage quantum-inspired physics to tackle complex optimization problems without requiring the extreme engineering challenges of fault-tolerant quantum computers. The New Zealand team's focus on scalability is especially significant, as many current quantum and quantum-inspired systems struggle to maintain performance advantages as problem sizes grow. While we await the arrival of fully fault-tolerant quantum computers, these hybrid optimization devices could deliver real-world value in logistics, finance, and materials science much sooner than traditional quantum computing timelines suggest.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — With the world’s first scalable, fault-tolerant quantum computer expected to be years away, a group of New Zealand researchers has developed an exciting hybrid device that could solve big, intractable problems sooner. Research Fellow, Dr Liam Quinn of Te Whai Ao — Dodd-Walls Centre is leading the work, considered of […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Washington Begins to Treat Quantum Technology as a Strategic Priority
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article signals a crucial shift as quantum technology moves from research curiosity to national security imperative in Washington's policy circles. The bipartisan engagement suggests lawmakers are beginning to understand quantum's dual nature as both an enormous economic opportunity and a potential threat to current cryptographic systems that underpin everything from financial transactions to government communications. What makes this particularly significant is the timing - as quantum computers edge closer to practical applications, the window for establishing regulatory frameworks, funding priorities, and international cooperation agreements is narrowing rapidly. The fact that these discussions are happening now, rather than after quantum advantage becomes widespread, suggests policymakers may finally be getting ahead of the technology curve for once.

Insider Brief Tuesday in Washington offered a clear picture of where quantum technology now stands, not as a future concept, but as a present responsibility. Meetings on Capitol Hill, organized by Optica, brought together lawmakers, federal agencies, and leaders from across the quantum ecosystem to focus on what comes next. The conversations were not about […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Google Paves a Two-Lane Quantum Roadmap by Adding Neutral Atom Systems
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article signals a major strategic shift for Google Quantum AI, which has built its quantum supremacy claims and current leadership position around superconducting qubits but is now hedging its bets by exploring neutral atom systems. The move reflects growing industry recognition that different quantum hardware approaches may excel at different types of problems, with neutral atoms potentially offering advantages in connectivity and scalability that could complement Google's superconducting expertise. By diversifying its quantum hardware portfolio, Google is positioning itself to capitalize on whichever technology proves most viable for near-term commercial applications, while also acknowledging that the path to fault-tolerant quantum computing may require multiple parallel approaches rather than betting everything on a single platform.

Insider Brief Google Quantum AI, which was a pioneer in the superconducting approach to quantum computing, announced that it is expanding beyond that core approach to add neutral atom quantum computing, a move the company says will accelerate progress toward commercially useful machines. The plan, discussed in a recent Google Quantum AI blog post, would […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Scalable quantum circuit generation for iterative ground state approximation using Majorana Propagation
item.description:
Curator's Take: This work tackles one of quantum computing's most promising near-term applications - finding ground states of molecular systems - by cleverly combining classical preprocessing with quantum circuit design. The researchers demonstrate their ADAPT-VMPE algorithm can generate practical quantum circuits for systems as large as 100 qubits while maintaining polynomial scaling, which is crucial for making quantum chemistry simulations feasible on near-term devices. What makes this particularly exciting is their focus on a real-world photosensitizer molecule used in cancer treatment, showing how quantum algorithms could eventually accelerate drug discovery and materials science. The theoretical guarantees on approximation error and the polynomial complexity bounds suggest this approach could bridge the gap between today's noisy intermediate-scale quantum devices and useful quantum advantage in molecular simulation.

We introduce the Adaptive Derivative-Assembled Pseudo-Trotter ansatz Variational Majorana Propagation Eigensolver (ADAPT-VMPE), a quantum-inspired classical algorithm that exploits Majorana Propagation (MP) to produce circuits for approximating the ground state of molecular Hamiltonians. Equipped with the theoretical guarantees of MP, which provide controllable bounds on the approximation error, ADAPT-VMPE offers an efficient and scalable approach for iterative ansatz construction. A theoretical analysis of the computational complexity demonstrates that it is polynomial in both the number of qubits and the number of iterations. We present an in-depth analysis of circuit construction strategies, analyzing their impact on convergence and provide practical guidance for efficient ansatz generation. Using ADAPT-VMPE, we construct up to 100-qubit ansätze for a strongly correlated photosensitizer currently undergoing human clinical trials for cancer treatment. Our results demonstrate that constant overlap with the ground state across system sizes can be reached in polynomial time with polynomially sized circuits.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum simulation of Motzkin spin chain with Rydberg atoms
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article presents a breakthrough pathway for experimentally realizing Motzkin spin chains, exotic quantum systems that have tantalized theorists for years due to their unusual entanglement properties that violate fundamental scaling laws. Unlike typical quantum systems where entanglement grows with the boundary area between regions, Motzkin chains exhibit logarithmic scaling that makes them notoriously difficult to simulate on classical computers, yet they hold deep connections to topological phases and even string theory through AdS/CFT correspondence. The researchers' proposed Rydberg atom platform offers a realistic experimental route to create these mathematical constructs in the lab, potentially opening doors to studying a whole new class of highly entangled quantum phases that could inform both fundamental physics and future quantum technologies. This work exemplifies how programmable quantum simulators are becoming powerful tools for exploring exotic quantum matter that exists at the edge of what's computationally tractable.

Motzkin spin chain is a well-known mathematical model with connections to symmetry-protected topological phases, such as the Haldane phase, as well as to concepts in the AdS/CFT correspondence. They exhibit highly entangled ground states that violate the area law and are exceptionally difficult to simulate with conventional numerical methods. Numerical simulations of the Motzkin ground state become further challenging at large system sizes due to their high-dimensional spin structure, rendering it a natural test bed for quantum simulation with ultra-cold systems. Here, we propose a Rydberg-atom based quantum simulation scheme that effectively realizes Motzkin spins using an experimentally accessible set of parameters. We show that the resulting effective Motzkin ground state reproduces the characteristic entanglement scaling and the block-structure properties of the reduced density matrix associated with the ideal Motzkin state. Our results establish a pathway toward a concrete experimental realization of Motzkin spins beyond purely mathematical constructions, opening avenues for exploring other similar exotic non-area-law entangled phases in programmable Rydberg simulators.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: NRFC Investment Accelerates SQC’s Atomic-Scale Manufacturing
item.description:
Curator's Take: This investment marks a significant milestone for Silicon Quantum Computing's unique atomic-scale manufacturing approach, which positions them as the sole company worldwide capable of fabricating quantum chips with atomic precision. The AUD$20 million from Australia's National Reconstruction Fund Corporation not only validates SQC's silicon-based quantum technology but also strengthens Australia's position in the global quantum race, particularly in the critical hardware manufacturing sector where precision at the atomic level remains one of the field's greatest technical challenges. What makes this particularly noteworthy is SQC's claim of already delivering quantum-enhanced AI products to enterprise and government clients today, suggesting their technology has moved beyond pure research into practical applications. This combination of manufacturing precision and commercial readiness could accelerate the timeline for practical quantum computing deployment, especially in applications where silicon-based quantum systems offer advantages over other qubit technologies.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — The National Reconstruction Fund Corporation (NRFC) has invested AUD$20 million in Silicon Quantum Computing (SQC), a global leader in the field of quantum computing. SQC is the only company in the world capable of manufacturing quantum chips with atomic precision while delivering quantum enhanced-AI products for enterprises and governments today. It is […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Global control via quantum actuators
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article introduces an elegant solution to one of quantum computing's biggest architectural challenges: how to perform selective quantum operations without needing individual control over every qubit. The proposed quantum actuators act like smart intermediaries that can temporarily store and release energy on command, allowing processors to activate specific multi-qubit gates even when using simple global control fields that affect all qubits simultaneously. This approach could dramatically simplify the control electronics needed for large-scale quantum computers while actually enhancing their connectivity and enabling long-range quantum operations between distant qubits. The connection to quantum thermodynamics through the "quantum battery" interpretation adds theoretical depth and suggests these actuators might serve dual purposes in future quantum processors.

We introduce the concept of quantum actuators as mediators for globally controlled quantum computation. Auxiliary quantum systems act as controllable elements that transiently store and release interaction energy, enabling the selective activation of multi-qubit gates within globally driven architectures. During compilation they remain passive and require no fine-grained local control, while during operation they allow for controlled activation of interactions and directional flow of quantum information. We provide a framework for embedding quantum actuators in globally controlled processors, showing how they enhance connectivity, enable long-range entangling operations, and bridge distant regions without increasing local control overhead. We discuss physical implementations and architectural strategies illustrating how these elements extend the capabilities of global-control schemes. A complementary interpretation in terms of quantum batteries naturally emerges, connecting global-control architectures with concepts from quantum thermodynamics while highlighting the distinct operational role of quantum actuators.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: UK Lawmaker Proposes U.K.-U.S. Quantum Partnership Can be a Strategic Counterweight to China
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights how quantum computing is increasingly viewed through the lens of national security and international competition, with policymakers recognizing that quantum supremacy could reshape global power dynamics. Tugendhat's proposal for deeper U.K.-U.S. quantum collaboration reflects growing concerns about China's substantial investments in quantum research and the strategic importance of maintaining Western leadership in this critical technology. The mention of Quantinuum as a model partnership underscores how the quantum industry is already operating across borders, suggesting that formal government partnerships could amplify existing commercial and research ties. This signals a shift from viewing quantum computing as purely a scientific endeavor to recognizing it as a cornerstone of future economic and military competitiveness.

Insider Brief A transatlantic partnership between the U.S. and the U.K. could serve as a fulcrum in the global effort to develop quantum computing, with Quantinuum positioned as a leading example of how combined capabilities may accelerate progress and shape geopolitical competition. In a Wall Street Journal opinion column, Tom Tugendhat, a member of the […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Study Finds More Qubits Yield Better Financial Optimization Outcomes
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article represents a crucial milestone in quantum computing's journey from laboratory curiosity to commercial viability, demonstrating that more qubits directly translate to better real-world performance in financial optimization problems. The collaboration between IonQ and Kipu Quantum shows quantum systems moving beyond proof-of-concept demonstrations to deliver measurable improvements over classical methods in portfolio optimization, one of the most promising near-term quantum applications. What makes this particularly significant is the clear scaling behavior observed when expanding from 36 to 64 qubits, suggesting that as quantum hardware continues to improve, financial institutions may soon see compelling reasons to adopt quantum solutions. This direct comparison with classical methods provides the kind of concrete evidence that enterprise decision-makers need to justify quantum computing investments.

Insider Brief Quantum hardware is beginning to show measurable gains on real financial problems, with larger qubit systems delivering better portfolio outcomes in direct comparisons with classical methods. A blog post, detailing results from a collaboration involving IonQ and Kipu Quantum, reports that increasing the size of executable quantum subproblems — from 36 to 64 […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: What Quantum Technology Actually is and Why it Matters
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article addresses a critical communication challenge facing the quantum industry: the tendency to oversimplify "quantum technology" as synonymous with quantum computing alone. While quantum computers capture headlines, quantum sensing and quantum communications are already delivering real-world applications in everything from medical imaging to secure financial transactions, yet they often get overlooked in mainstream discussions. As governments worldwide develop quantum strategies and allocate billions in funding, having precise terminology becomes essential for effective policy-making and public understanding. The piece serves as a valuable primer for distinguishing between quantum computing's future promises and the quantum technologies already transforming industries today.

Insider Brief The term “quantum technology” has entered mainstream discourse with increasing frequency, appearing in government policy documents, corporate strategy announcements, venture capital portfolios, and media headlines. However, the phrase often gets used imprecisely, sometimes referring exclusively to quantum computing while ignoring equally transformative developments in sensing and communications. Quantum technology is not a single […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Bell Experiments Revisited: A Numerical Approach Based on De Broglie--Bohm Theory
item.description:
Curator's Take: This pedagogical study offers a fascinating numerical demonstration of how the de Broglie-Bohm interpretation of quantum mechanics can reproduce the famous Bell inequality violations that seem to rule out local hidden variable theories. By providing explicit simulations of particle trajectories and spin dynamics within this deterministic framework, the researchers bridge a crucial gap between abstract quantum theory and concrete visualization of entanglement phenomena. While de Broglie-Bohm theory remains a minority interpretation among physicists due to its requirement of nonlocal interactions, this work serves as an excellent educational tool for understanding how different interpretations can yield identical experimental predictions while maintaining fundamentally different pictures of physical reality. The numerical approach makes these subtle conceptual distinctions more accessible to students and researchers grappling with the foundational puzzles of quantum mechanics.

We present a complete and rigorous model of an EPR--Bell-type experiment within the framework of the de Broglie--Bohm theory. The purpose of this work is to show explicitly how a deterministic hidden-variable theory can reproduce all quantum-mechanical predictions, including the violation of Bell inequalities. Combining analytical arguments with numerical simulations, our approach offers a unified and transparent illustration of the central ingredients of de Broglie--Bohm theory, including particle trajectories, spin dynamics, and quantum entanglement. The model is designed to be pedagogical and self-contained, making it suitable for readers seeking a concrete understanding of how a nonlocal hidden-variable theory can describe the EPR--Bell experiment and illustrate Bell's theorem.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly
